text: The surgeon begins the operation with optical entry in the right upper quadrant. Three ports are placed medial to the right linea semilunaris. The insertion of the port in the right lower quadrant is shown, highlighting the limited space available. The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon navigates the confined lower abdominal area with careful micro-movements to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon begins the dissection in the upper abdomen, where there is slightly more room, focusing on completing the dissection of the retro-rectus space. Both instruments are visible in the same view, facilitating the procedure. The crossover is initiated while the CO2 is still contained, making it easier to work in this space.
The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity during the crossover phase of the procedure, revealing the incisional hernia site with minimal preperitoneal fat surrounding it.

In the upper abdomen, the surgeon navigates through a plane with more pre-peritoneal fat, entering the intra-abdominal space. Adhesions are being carefully dissected, followed by the removal of the pseudosac to address the defect and anticipate a large posterior gap.

The surgeon finishes the crossover in the lower abdomen by cutting the patient's right posterior rectal sheath to access the preperitoneal space below the umbilicus. The video shows a very tight working space in this area.

The surgeon completes the lysis of adhesions within the hernia sac and enters the left retrorectus space. The dissection is performed both below and above the hernia defect, using a grasper to guide the incision in the posterior rectus sheath, progressing from left to right.

The surgeon divides the round ligament in the left pelvis. Due to the large size of the hernia defect, a transversus abdominis release (TAR) is initiated at the arcuate line, starting from the bottom up to achieve sufficient anterior release for tension-free closure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from behind the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring safe division of the sheath while avoiding pulling the anterior lamella of the internal oblique into the transection line. The upper transversus abdominis release (TAR) is initiated, with dissection under the diaphragm, dividing the posterior rectus sheath, the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, transverse abdominis, and the diaphragm's posterior contribution. The TAR is completed by connecting the upper and lower dissections, revealing the defect.

The surgeon encounters difficulty visualizing the ipsilateral linea alba while focusing on closing the posterior layer of the abdominal wall. The visible defect measures approximately nine by fifteen centimeters, highlighting the limited workspace available for closure.

The surgeon uses a pseudo-sac held in the left hand to patch a large defect, as the posterior sheath edges cannot be brought together despite performing a left-sided TAR. The pseudo-sac effectively fills the gap, preventing the need for alternative patches or a right-sided TAR.

The surgeon closes the posterior gap using a three-hole V-lock 180 suture, completing the final defect closure. The plan involves closing the diastasis and repositioning the camera to the right upper quadrant port for a top-down view to address the anterior defect.

The surgeon shifts instruments to the left upper quadrant port, providing a clear view of the edge of the mesh. The right hand is repositioned for easier suturing, while the left hand is pinned against the side due to proximity to the ipsilateral edge, which is less than four centimeters away.

The surgeon places a large piece of mesh to cover the defect, extending beyond the area of the transversus abdominis release (TAR) to cover the left linea semilunaris. The workspace is limited, with the roof of the surgical field close to the bow, highlighting the precision of robotic assistance.
